regressions.reasoning: The failure is a total management-API connection failure (every query fails identically), occurring at the very first regression check right after stage_08's own oracle teardown itself timed out waiting on kubectl — pointing to transient cluster/API-server stress at that moment, not a deliberate later-stage change. Stage_05's regression, which re-verifies the same /app vhost and app-queue still exist with messages after the version upgrade, passed cleanly, confirming stage_01's underlying state was never actually destroyed.
regressions.output:
$ python3 cases/rabbitmq/classic_queue/oracle/oracle.py
Classic queue verification failed:
  - Failed to query cluster nodes via management API (base=https://rabbitmq:15671): Command '['kubectl', '-n', 'karma-rabbitmq-security-upgrade-v2-01-ca2a415e-cluster-a', 'exec', 'oracle-client', '--', 'curl', '-sk', '--connect-timeout', '5', '--max-time', '25', '-u', 'admin:adminpass', 'https://rabbitmq:15671/api/nodes']' returned non-zero exit status 1.: ''
  - Failed to query policies for /app
  - Failed to query app-user permissions for /app
  - Failed to query app-queue state

regressions.reasoning: No stage after stage_07 (only stage_08, an unrelated Prometheus-scraping task) has any business touching RabbitMQ users, vhosts, or permissions, so nothing in the workflow legitimately supersedes stage_07's fix. RabbitMQ in this benchmark runs on non-persistent (emptyDir) storage, so the most likely cause is the agent restarting/recreating the RabbitMQ pods while wiring up the Prometheus plugin in stage_08 instead of enabling it at runtime without a restart — wiping the mnesia-held users/permissions stage_07 had correctly set. That's careless agent action breaking prior state, i.e. a real regression.

  - ops-user does not have full permissions on /ops

summary: all 8 stages passed; 2 regression-sweep failure(s): 1 real regression(s), 1 false positive(s) -> score 87.5.
